Clinical notes and X-ray report not indicative of surgery.

clinical_key_pointers: Congenital club foot is a complex deformity that is difficult to correct and has a tendency to recur until the age of six or seven years, often associated with incomplete correction.
Untreated, children with clubfoot walk on the sides and/or top of their foot, resulting in callus formation.
The deformity has four components: Equinius, varus, adductus and cavus.
The most recognizable congenital foot deformity, clubfoot, is characterized by plantar flexion of the ankle, inversion of the foot, and adduction of the forefoot.

Clubfoot is a complex foot deformity comprised of five fixed deformities: Hindfoot (Equinus foot position due to short Achilles tendon, Varus position/supination of calcaneus), Forefoot (Adductus/Pigeon toe/false clubfoot/metatarsus adductus, Supinatus/inversion of forefoot), and Cavus (high arch).
Surgery for clubfoot is indicated when a plateau has been reached in nonoperative treatment.
Manipulative treatment (Ponseti-method with serial casting) and Foot abduction brace (Ponseti brace) are also discussed in the context of overall corrections.
surgical_options: Achilles tenotomy (lengthening the Achilles tendon with a Z-shaped suture)
Ilizarov distraction (implied by procedure name and image)
JESS Fixator (implied by procedure name and image)
